title: The CSS Rendering Pipeline: Reflow vs Repaint & Why `transform` Is Cheap
description: Follow a pixel from CSS to screen: **Style → Layout → Paint → Composite**. See exactly what makes the browser re-run *Layout* (reflow) versus just *Paint* (repaint), why `transform` and `opacity` ride the compositor and skip both, and how reading `offsetHeight` at the wrong moment forces a synchronous layout. The rendering-side companion to the event-loop quiz — same main thread, the other half of its story. Every claim is grounded in the browser's documented rendering steps.

questions.title: What is the correct order of the browser's rendering pipeline?
questions.description: After JavaScript has run and the DOM and styles are ready, the browser turns them into pixels in four main steps.

options.text: **Style** → **Layout** → **Paint** → **Composite**
**Layout** → **Style** → **Paint** → **Composite**
**Style** → **Paint** → **Layout** → **Composite**
**Style** → **Layout** → **Composite** → **Paint**

questions.explanation:
The browser always works in this fixed order:

1. **Style (Recalculate Style)** — match CSS selectors to elements and compute the final value of every property for every node.
2. **Layout (Reflow)** — calculate the *geometry*: how big each box is and where it sits.
3. **Paint** — fill in pixels: text, colors, borders, shadows, images — drawn onto layers.
4. **Composite** — assemble those layers and draw them to the screen in the correct order.

The sequence is not arbitrary. You cannot know an element's size (*Layout*) until you know its styles (*Style*), and you cannot fill its pixels (*Paint*) until you know its size. *Composite* is last because it merely arranges what *Paint* already produced. In DevTools you will also see a *JavaScript* slice drawn before *Style*, since script is the usual trigger that kicks the whole pipeline off.

questions.title: What does the **Style** (Recalculate Style) step actually compute?
questions.description: DevTools shows a *Recalculate Style* event before any layout work.

options.text: It matches CSS rules to elements and resolves the final **computed value** of every property — but positions nothing yet.
It measures each element's width, height, and position on the page.
It fills in the pixels for text and backgrounds.
It downloads and parses the stylesheet into the CSSOM.

questions.explanation:
Style turns *rules* into *resolved styles*. The browser walks the cascade — selector matching, specificity, inheritance — and produces one fully **computed style** per element (for example `font-size: 16px`, not `1em`). It figures out *what* an element should look like, never *where* it goes.

- Measuring size and position is the next step, **Layout**.
- Filling pixels is **Paint**.
- Parsing CSS text into the **CSSOM** happens earlier, while the page loads; *Recalculate Style* consumes that CSSOM, it does not build it.

This split is why a selector-heavy stylesheet can make *Style* expensive even on a page whose layout never moves — matching is real work, independent of geometry.

questions.title: Which pipeline steps must run on each frame of the `width` transition?

options.text: **Layout, then Paint, then Composite** — changing geometry forces all three.
**Layout only** — the pixels are unchanged, so Paint and Composite are skipped.
**Composite only** — like `transform`, `width` is handled directly by the GPU.
**Paint, then Composite** — `width` is a paint property, so Layout is skipped.

questions.explanation:
`width` is a **layout** property, and Layout is never free: once geometry changes, the browser *must* re-Paint the affected area (the box is a different size, so its old pixels are wrong) and then *must* re-Composite. So a `width` animation pays the full pipeline — **Layout → Paint → Composite** — on every single frame.

This is the key *downstream* rule:

- Change a **layout** property → Layout **+** Paint **+** Composite.
- Change a **paint-only** property → Paint **+** Composite (Layout skipped).
- Change a **composite-only** property → Composite only (Layout *and* Paint skipped).

You can skip steps from the *front* of the pipeline, never from the *back*. That single fact is why animating `width`, `top`, or `margin` is among the most expensive things you can do per frame — and why the next questions reach for `transform` instead.

questions.title: What has Firefox (Gecko) historically called the step Chrome's DevTools labels *Layout*?
questions.description: It's the step that recomputes geometry. Answer with one word.
questions.explanation:
**Reflow** is Gecko's name for what Chrome's pipeline calls **Layout**: recomputing where every box sits and how large it is. The two words are interchangeable, and you will meet both in articles and profilers.

The companion term is **repaint** — redrawing pixels *without* recomputing geometry (the *Paint* step). So 'reflow vs repaint' is simply 'Layout vs Paint' in older vocabulary. Keeping the pairing straight is the whole mental model: a **reflow always causes a repaint**, but a **repaint does not require a reflow**.

questions.title: Which change triggers a **repaint** but *no* reflow?
questions.description: Each change below is applied to a button that keeps exactly the same size and position.

options.text: `background-color: red` → `background-color: blue`
`display: inline` → `display: block`
`font-size: 14px` → `font-size: 18px`
`padding: 8px` → `padding: 16px`

questions.explanation:
`background-color` is a **paint-only** property: it changes which pixels are drawn but not the box's size or position, so the browser skips **Layout** entirely and runs only **Paint → Composite**. That is a textbook *repaint without reflow*.

The other three all alter geometry, forcing a reflow:

- `display: inline → block` changes the box type and how it flows among siblings.
- `font-size` changes text metrics, so line boxes and the element's size change.
- `padding` changes the box's dimensions directly.

Other common paint-only properties include `color`, `visibility`, `box-shadow`, `border-color`, `border-radius`, `outline`, and `background-image`. They are cheaper than layout changes, but — unlike `transform`/`opacity` — they still cost a genuine Paint.

questions.title: Which of these property changes force a **Layout (reflow)**?
questions.description: A reflow means the browser must recompute size or position — not merely repaint. Select **all** that apply.

options.text: `height: auto` → `height: 300px`
`color: black` → `color: gray`
`top: 0` → `top: 40px` on a `position: absolute` element
`transform: none` → `transform: translateY(40px)`
`font-weight: 400` → `font-weight: 700`
`visibility: visible` → `visibility: hidden`

questions.explanation:
Layout is about **geometry** — size, position, and anything that moves other boxes. The three that reflow:

- **`height`** changes the box's size directly.
- **`top` on a positioned element** moves it through the layout system, recomputing its position.
- **`font-weight`** changes glyph advance widths, so text re-wraps and line boxes resize — a real reflow that is easy to overlook.

The three that do **not** reflow:

- **`color`** is paint-only.
- **`visibility: hidden`** leaves the element's box exactly where it was (it still occupies space) — only its pixels change, so it is a repaint. By contrast `display: none` *would* reflow, because it removes the box from flow.
- **`transform: translateY()`** is the star of this quiz: composite-only, skipping *both* Layout and Paint.

Notice the trap pairs — `visibility: hidden` vs `display: none`, and `translateY()` vs `top` — each looks similar but lands in a different pipeline bucket.

questions.title: Why are `transform` and `opacity` the two 'cheap' properties to animate?

options.text: Once the element is on its own compositor layer, the GPU can move/fade the *already-painted* layer — so the browser skips both **Layout** and **Paint**.
They are simpler CSS values, so the **Style** step parses them faster.
The browser caps their updates to 30fps, halving the work per second.
They are applied by the operating system's window manager, bypassing the browser entirely.

questions.explanation:
`transform` (translate, scale, rotate, skew) and `opacity` are the canonical **composite-only** properties. If the element has been promoted to its own **compositor layer**, that layer is painted into a GPU texture *once*; animating transform or opacity then just hands the compositor a new matrix or alpha value, and the GPU re-blends the existing texture. No box is re-measured (**no Layout**) and no pixels are re-filled (**no Paint**) — only **Composite** runs.

That is why a `transform: translateX()` animation can hold 60fps where an equivalent `left` animation janks: `left` pays Layout + Paint + Composite every frame, while `transform` pays Composite alone.

One caveat worth knowing: the cheapness depends on the element being on its **own layer**. If it is not, the browser may still repaint to apply the change — which is exactly what `will-change` (a later question) is for.

questions.title: Which way of sliding the card 100px down is cheaper per frame, and why?

options.text: **B is cheaper.** `top` reruns Layout + Paint + Composite each frame; `translateY` runs Composite only.
**A is cheaper.** `top` is a native layout property, so the browser optimizes it more aggressively than transforms.
They are identical — both ultimately move the element, so the browser does the same work.
**B is cheaper**, but only because `translateY` rounds to whole pixels and skips sub-pixel rendering.

questions.explanation:
Visually identical, structurally worlds apart.

- **A (`top`)** is a layout property. Every animation frame recomputes the card's position (**Layout**), re-fills its pixels (**Paint**), then **Composites** — the full pipeline, on the main thread, roughly 60 times a second.
- **B (`translateY`)** is composite-only. The card is painted once; each frame just hands the compositor a new translation matrix. **No Layout, no Paint** — only **Composite**, largely on the GPU.

The rule of thumb for movement animations: **prefer `transform: translate()` over `top`/`left`/`margin`.** The same holds for fades: prefer `opacity` over toggling `visibility` or background alpha. You get the same motion for a fraction of the per-frame cost — and, as the next question shows, on a thread that JavaScript cannot easily block.

questions.title: A 500ms synchronous task blocks the main thread. Which animation keeps moving?

options.text: The **`transform`** transition keeps animating (compositor thread); the **`left`** animation freezes (it needs the blocked main thread).
Both freeze — a blocked main thread stops all rendering on the page.
Both keep moving — animations always run on a separate thread from JavaScript.
The **`left`** animation keeps moving; the **`transform`** one freezes.

questions.explanation:
This is where the rendering pipeline meets the event loop. A composited `transform`/`opacity` animation is handed to the **compositor thread**, which runs independently of the main thread. So even while the `while` loop monopolizes the main thread, the compositor keeps re-blending the spinner's layer — it stays smooth.

The `requestAnimationFrame` + `el.style.left` animation lives entirely on the **main thread**: each frame needs JavaScript to run *and* a Layout/Paint pass. While the long task holds the thread, none of that can happen, so it freezes — then jumps to its new position once the task ends.

This is the rendering-side payoff of 'don't block the event loop': a long synchronous task stalls JavaScript, Layout, and Paint together, but a compositor-only animation sails right through. It is also the strongest practical argument for animating `transform` instead of `top`/`left`.

questions.title: What does `will-change: transform` actually do — and what is the catch?

options.text: It hints the browser to promote the element to its **own compositor layer ahead of time**, so the animation starts without a setup hitch — but overusing it wastes memory.
It makes every property on the element animate faster by default.
It forces an immediate Layout so future transforms are pre-measured.
It is purely documentation for other developers; the browser ignores it.

questions.explanation:
`will-change` is a *hint*: 'I am about to animate `transform`, please get ready.' The browser responds by promoting the element to its **own layer** in advance, so the moment the animation begins there is no first-frame hitch from creating a layer and doing a fresh paint.

The catch is cost. Each promoted layer consumes GPU memory and bookkeeping, so MDN's guidance is explicit: use it **sparingly** — ideally add it shortly before the animation and remove it afterward, never slap it on `*` or leave it on permanently. Over-promotion can make a page *slower* and balloon memory use.

The old-school equivalent is the 'null transform hack', `transform: translateZ(0)` (or `translate3d(0, 0, 0)`), which forces a layer as a side effect. `will-change` is the modern, explicit way to ask for the same thing. Neither triggers a Layout — they prepare the **compositor**, not the geometry.

questions.title: Which lines force a **synchronous layout** right after a style write?
questions.description: A style write in JS just dirtied the layout. "Force" means the browser must stop and recompute layout *immediately* to return a correct value. Select **all** that apply.

options.text: `const h = box.offsetHeight;`
`const r = box.getBoundingClientRect();`
`box.style.height = '200px';`
`const w = getComputedStyle(box).width;`
`const t = box.scrollTop;`
`box.classList.add('open');`

questions.explanation:
After a write dirties the layout, the browser would *prefer* to batch and recompute once before the next frame. But the instant you **read a value that depends on up-to-date geometry**, it is forced to recompute *right now* to give you an accurate answer. That is a **forced synchronous layout** (a.k.a. forced reflow).

The four readers all demand live geometry:

- `offsetHeight`, `offsetWidth`, `offsetTop`, …
- `getBoundingClientRect()`
- `getComputedStyle(...)` of a layout-dependent property
- `scrollTop`, `scrollHeight`, `scrollLeft`, …

The two **writes** do *not* force layout — they only *invalidate* it (mark it dirty), and the work is deferred:

- `box.style.height = '200px'`
- `box.classList.add('open')`

The real danger is **interleaving** a write and then a read, the subject of the next question. Paul Irish maintains a well-known list of exactly which properties trip this.
questions.references: [Paul Irish: What forces layout / reflow](https://gist.github.com/paulirish/5d52fb081b3570c81e3a) · [web.dev: Avoid large, complex layouts and layout thrashing](https://web.dev/articles/avoid-large-complex-layouts-and-layout-thrashing)

questions.title: Why is this loop slow, and what is the one-line fix?

options.text: Each iteration **writes** a width (dirtying layout) then **reads** `box.offsetWidth` (forcing a re-layout) — so it pays roughly N forced layouts. Fix: read `box.offsetWidth` **once before** the loop.
`offsetWidth` is a slow DOM property; cache it in a variable *inside* the loop to speed it up.
Setting `style.width` in a loop leaks memory; use a `DocumentFragment` instead.
The loop is fine; 200 iterations is too few to matter, so no change is needed.

questions.explanation:
This is the classic **layout thrashing** pattern: a **read-after-write** loop. The write `paragraphs[i].style.width = ...` dirties the document's layout; the very next iteration reads `box.offsetWidth`, forcing the browser to flush a **synchronous layout** to return a correct number. Repeat 200 times and you get roughly 200 forced layouts in a single frame.

The fix is to **separate reads from writes** — hoist the read out of the loop so layout is computed at most once:

```js
const width = box.offsetWidth; // one read, one (possible) layout
for (let i = 0; i < paragraphs.length; i++) {
  paragraphs[i].style.width = width + 'px'; // writes only; layout deferred to one pass
}
```

Now the writes just queue up and the browser does a single layout before the next paint. The general principle, popularized by helpers like FastDOM: **batch all your reads, then all your writes** — never alternate them. Caching `offsetWidth` *inside* the loop would not help, because each write re-dirties it; the read must move *out*.
questions.references: [web.dev: Avoid large, complex layouts and layout thrashing](https://web.dev/articles/avoid-large-complex-layouts-and-layout-thrashing) · [Paul Irish: What forces layout / reflow](https://gist.github.com/paulirish/5d52fb081b3570c81e3a)

questions.title: Rounded to two decimals, how many milliseconds is the 60fps frame budget?
questions.description: To render smoothly at 60fps, the browser aims to produce a new frame every `1000 / 60` ms. Enter the number only.
questions.explanation:
`1000 ms / 60 frames ≈ 16.67 ms` per frame. Every step in this quiz — Style, Layout, Paint, Composite, plus any JavaScript — has to fit inside that window, or the frame is late and the user sees jank.

In practice the usable budget is even tighter: the browser needs a few milliseconds of its own housekeeping per frame, so the common working guidance is to keep your JS + rendering work under roughly **10 ms**. This is precisely why composite-only animations matter — skipping Layout and Paint can be the difference between fitting in 16.67 ms and missing it.

And the bar keeps rising: on a 120Hz display the budget halves to `1000 / 120 ≈ 8.33 ms`, making those savings matter even more.

questions.title: Which statements about the rendering pipeline are true?

options.text: Changing only `transform` or `opacity` can skip both Layout and Paint.
A reflow always causes a repaint, but a repaint does not require a reflow.
Reading `offsetHeight` can force a synchronous layout.
`background-color` changes trigger a reflow.

questions.explanation:
Three hold up:

- **`transform`/`opacity`** are composite-only: on their own layer they skip **Layout** and **Paint**, running only **Composite**.
- **Reflow implies repaint, but not the reverse.** A geometry change forces the browser to re-Paint the moved or resized area, so Layout always drags Paint along; a paint-only change (like `color`) skips Layout. You can drop steps from the *front* of the pipeline, never the *back*.
- **`offsetHeight`** (and friends like `getBoundingClientRect()`) read live geometry, forcing a synchronous layout when the layout is dirty.

The false statement: **`background-color` is paint-only** — it changes pixels, not geometry, so it triggers a repaint, never a reflow. Confusing 'changes how it looks' with 'changes where things are' is the single most common pipeline misconception.
